metadata.summary: Go backend pattern: cmd/ + internal/ layout, consumer-side interfaces, context propagation, typed errors, worker pool concurrency.

content.title: Go Backend
content.summary: Production-grade Go backend pattern using the standard cmd/ + internal/ project layout with Gin or Echo routers. Key conventions: interfaces defined at the consumer side, context.Context as first arg on all I/O functions, typed AppError mapped to HTTP via middleware, worker-pool concurrency via a Pool struct with channel-based job queue.

why.p: The internal/ boundary prevents circular imports and keeps domain packages isolated. Consumer-side interfaces (service defines UserRepository, repository provides the impl) enable mockable unit tests without reflection-based mocking. Typed errors with Unwrap() let callers use errors.Is / errors.As for clean control flow. Worker pool decouples job submission from execution and supports graceful shutdown via context.Context.

list.item: Greenfield Go service scaffolding with cmd/ + internal/handler/service/repository layers.
Adding endpoints to an existing Gin/Echo project following this layout.
Generating typed AppError taxonomies and error-handler middleware.
Building worker-pool / fan-out-fan-in glue code around a typed Job interface.
Non-standard layouts (Hex / Clean / monorepo multi-module) — import paths will be wrong.
Performance-critical hot paths needing sync.Pool or escape-analysis awareness.
Cgo / unsafe / kernel-bypass code — out of scope.
Generics-heavy domain libraries — examples use pre-generics style.

rule.statement: Use the standard Go layout: cmd/api/main.go for entry point, internal/{handler,service,repository,model,middleware,config} for domain code, pkg/ for public packages, migrations/ for DB migrations.
rule.rationale: internal/ prevents external imports, enforcing encapsulation. cmd/ supports multiple binaries from one module.

example.code: project/ ├── cmd/api/main.go ├── internal/ │ ├── handler/ │ ├── service/ │ ├── repository/ │ ├── model/ │ ├── middleware/ │ └── config/ ├── pkg/ ├── migrations/ ├── go.mod └── go.sum

rule.statement: Define interfaces in the package that consumes them, not in the package that provides the implementation. service/ defines UserRepository; repository/ provides the implementation.
rule.rationale: This is the Go idiom. Interfaces at the consumer side break import cycles and make mocking straightforward — the test provides a mock that satisfies the interface without importing the real impl.

rule.statement: Use separate *Request structs for HTTP request binding. Never bind directly into domain model structs.
rule.rationale: Domain models may have fields that must never be set by external input (IDs, timestamps, internal flags). DTO separation enforces this boundary.

rule.statement: Keep cmd/api/main.go thin: only wiring (config → deps → router → server). No business logic.
rule.rationale: Agents bloat main.go when not constrained. Thin main makes each layer independently testable.

rule.statement: Pass context.Context as the first argument of every exported function that does I/O. Never capture gin.Context inside a goroutine — derive context first.
rule.rationale: Context carries cancellation, deadlines, and trace IDs. Capturing gin.Context in a goroutine is a data race and causes request-context leaks.

rule.statement: Use Gin with small, single-purpose middlewares. Never put business logic in middleware.
rule.rationale: Small middlewares compose cleanly and are easier for agents to understand and extend.

rule.statement: Use ShouldBindJSON for Gin (returns error) instead of BindJSON (aborts on error). Use binding struct tags for validation.
rule.rationale: ShouldBind lets handlers return custom error responses; BindJSON writes a 400 and aborts, bypassing the ErrorHandler middleware.

rule.statement: Use a single ErrorHandler middleware that maps AppError to HTTP status. Handlers call c.Error(err) and return — never call c.JSON directly for errors.
rule.rationale: Centralizing error mapping ensures consistent error response shape and avoids duplicating HTTP status logic in every handler.

rule.statement: Use the Pool struct with a buffered jobs channel and context-aware workers. Pool.Submit must select on both the jobs channel and ctx.Done() to avoid deadlock under cancellation.
rule.rationale: Blocking submit on a full buffer without ctx awareness deadlocks when the caller is cancelled. The select pattern drains cleanly.

rule.statement: Use runtime.NumCPU() for default worker count. Close the jobs channel after all jobs are sent; let workers drain naturally.
rule.rationale: Closing the channel signals workers to exit their range loop cleanly without an explicit stop signal.

template.purpose: AppError struct with sentinel errors and constructors.
CI script: verify required internal/ dirs exist and internal/ is not imported from outside.
